---
title: "Dittonomics"
subtitle: "Sebastian Ritter's public operating manual for a Ditto-style Codex economics workflow"
---

::: {.columns .ditto-hero-grid}
::: {.column width="30%"}
![](assets/ditto-mascot.svg){fig-alt="Dittonomics mascot: a smiling purple blob inspired by Ditto." .ditto-hero}
:::

::: {.column .ditto-hero-copy width="70%"}
Dittonomics is my public operating manual for doing economics research with Codex.

The Ditto idea is intentional: this system keeps copying useful traits from upstream workflows, then reorganizes them into one portable setup that I can keep extending over time and that colleagues can learn without digging through every starter file. The website is the public manual. The repo is the starter kit. Your local install is the live working system.
:::
:::

::: {.ditto-fast-path}
## Fork / Install / Customize

Start here if you want to use Dittonomics yourself or roll it out for coauthors:

- **Open the public manual**: [sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics](https://sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics/)
- **Fork the GitHub repo**: [github.com/sebastianritterg/dittonomics](https://github.com/sebastianritterg/dittonomics)
- **Install the starter locally**: copy the repo's `.codex/` into your own `~/.codex/`
- **Add your own writing voice**: copy the `voice/` templates into `~/voice/` and adapt them privately
- **Customize per project**: keep paper-specific rules in each repo's `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, and `.agents/skills/`
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## What This Manual Is For

This site is written for two audiences:

- Me (Sebastian Ritter), as the person actively evolving the system and publishing the public manual
- colleagues, coauthors, and future users who need a practical guide they can actually work from
:::

## What Dittonomics Gives You

- an explicit ideation phase before discovery
- a research pipeline with explicit phases
- worker-critic separation across the stack
- Codex-native skills instead of Claude slash-command assumptions
- a forkable public `.codex/` starter bundle
- optional voice files for writing-sensitive workflows
- a strategic post-submission revision workflow with roadmap planning and DAG validation
- explicit maintenance tools such as `checkpoint`, `resume-context`, and `verify-edit`
- a website manual and cheat sheet you can keep open while you work

## Use This Site Like A Roadmap

This site is meant to help you use the system correctly, not just understand that it exists.

Use the pages in this order:

1. **Quick Start** when you need the big picture and the install split
2. **User Guide** when you want the practical step-by-step workflow from idea to submission
3. **Reference** when you just need the right skill, utility, or prompt quickly
4. **Agents** when you want to know who gets activated and why
5. **Customization** when you want to fork the system, add voice, or onboard colleagues
6. **Architecture** when you want to understand how the layers fit together under the hood

If you only keep one page open during the day, keep **Reference** open. If you only read one page before using the system properly, read **User Guide**.

## Research Roadmap At A Glance

::: {.ditto-roadmap}
::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 1. Ideate
Use `$clo-ideate`

Turn a topic, mechanism, or dataset hunch into real research questions.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 2. Discover
Use `$clo-discover`

Map the literature, data, and framing once a candidate question exists.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 3. Strategize
Use `$clo-strategize`

Lock in the estimand, design, threats, and robustness logic before coding.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 4. Analyze
Use `$clo-analyze`

Build the empirical pipeline, outputs, and handoff artifacts.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 5. Write
Use `$clo-write`

Draft the paper once the evidence and design are stable enough to narrate.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 6. Review
Use `$clo-review`

Pressure-test the draft before treating it as ready.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 7. Revise
Use `$clo-revise`

When reports arrive, build the roadmap first and edit second.
:::

::: {.ditto-roadmap-step}
### 8. Submit / Talk
Use `$clo-submit` or `$clo-talk`

Close the loop with verification or convert the paper into a presentation.
:::
:::

## Research Roadmap In Words

This is the recommended order of use for most economics projects:

1. `Ideate` with `$clo-ideate` when the idea is still vague
2. `Discover` with `$clo-discover` once you have a candidate question
3. `Strategize` with `$clo-strategize` before writing serious code
4. `Analyze` with `$clo-analyze` to build the empirical pipeline
5. `Write` with `$clo-write` once the outputs and design are stable enough
6. `Review` with `$clo-review` before treating the draft as real
7. `Revise` with `$clo-revise` when editor or referee reports arrive
8. `Submit` with `$clo-submit` when the package is close to done
9. `Talk` with `$clo-talk` when the paper needs a presentation version

That is the main mental model for the whole system: each workflow skill owns a stage, and the agents sit underneath that stage rather than replacing it.

## Start In 20 Minutes

### 1. Fork or clone the repo

The public repo contains three things you will reuse repeatedly:

- the website manual in `guide/` and `docs/`
- the public starter bundle in `.codex/`
- the public voice templates in `voice/`

### 2. Install the starter bundle locally

Copy or adapt the repo's `.codex/` into your own `~/.codex/`.

That gives you:

- `AGENTS.md`
- `skills/`
- `agents/`
- workflow references
- utility scripts

### 3. Add an optional voice layer

If you want writing to preserve your own prose register, copy the files in `voice/` into `~/voice/` and fill them in privately.

If you do not need a voice layer, skip it.

### 4. Open a research repo with Codex

In a working project repo, add:

- `AGENTS.md`
- optional `AGENTS.override.md`
- optional repo-local skills in `.agents/skills/`
- `explorations/` when you need a prototype sandbox

### 5. Start with ideation when the question is still fuzzy

Before discovery, use:

- `use $clo-ideate session`

That step helps you:

- turn a topic or dataset hunch into candidate research questions
- check the closest-paper threat early
- decide whether to pursue, refine, park, or kill the idea

### 6. Use the daily loop

Morning:

- `use $clo-research-tools resume-context`

During work:

- call the workflow skill that matches the task

End of day:

- `use $clo-research-tools checkpoint`

## First Prompts To Try

```text
Use $clo-ideate session on this policy idea and turn it into ranked research questions before discovery.
```

```text
Use $clo-discover lit to map the literature on teacher value-added and school assignment.
```

```text
Use $clo-strategize on this research question and propose the main design plus the referee-proof robustness plan.
```

```text
Use $clo-write intro with current-draft mode and keep econ-intro-writing in charge of structure.
```

```text
Use $clo-review --peer on the current draft and tell me the highest-risk issues first.
```

```text
Use $clo-revise roadmap on these referee reports and the submitted paper. Build the execution blocks before we start editing.
```

```text
Use $clo-research-tools checkpoint for this repo. Capture today's goal, what we finished, blockers, important files, and the best next step.
```

## How The Site Is Organized

- **Quick Start**: what Dittonomics is and how to get running quickly
- **User Guide**: the practical roadmap for how to use the system of skills and agents properly for economics research
- **Agents**: who does what and when the system dispatches each role
- **Architecture**: how the website, starter bundle, local install, repo overlays, and state files fit together
- **Customization**: how to install your own stack, set up voice, and onboard colleagues
- **Reference**: the cheat sheet for skills, utilities, agents, folders, and recurring prompts

## What Dittonomics Builds On

Dittonomics is a remix, not a one-source clone.

The current public system draws especially from:

- [Clo-Author](https://hsantanna.org/clo-author/) for the overall research-workflow architecture
- [my_claude_skills](https://github.com/dariia-m/my_claude_skills) for explicit skill patterns and reusable operational helpers
- [claudeblattman](https://github.com/chrisblattman/claudeblattman) for economics-facing workflow instincts and practical researcher ergonomics
- [strategic-revision](https://github.com/jusi-aalto/strategic-revision) for post-submission revision planning, task decomposition, and dependency logic
- [research-companion](https://github.com/andrehuang/research-companion/) for research-stage orchestration ideas that fit both Codex and Claude Code settings
- [idea-evaluation-pipeline](https://github.com/alejandroll10/idea-evaluation-pipeline) for screening seed ideas and turning them into sharper research questions

The Ditto rule still applies: more useful traits can be added later, but they should enter the system visibly through documented skills, agents, and tutorial pages.

## Public Home

The public website is:

- [https://sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics/](https://sebastianritterg.github.io/dittonomics/)

Keep this site open while you work. It is meant to be the everyday operating manual and cheat sheet for the system, especially when you or a colleague forget which skill to call next.

## How To Fork Dittonomics

If you want your own version of the system:

1. fork the repo on GitHub
2. clone your fork locally
3. copy or adapt the repo's `.codex/` starter bundle into your own `~/.codex/`
4. optionally copy the `voice/` templates into your private `~/voice/`
5. keep repo-specific rules in each project's `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, and `.agents/skills/`
6. use this website as the shared manual for your own adaptation

The practical split is:

- fork the repo when you want your own public or shared Dittonomics variant
- copy the starter bundle when you want the user-level install on your machine
- use repo overlays when a specific paper or project needs local rules

## Public Repo vs Local Install

| Layer | Role |
|---|---|
| `guide/` | source for the website manual |
| `docs/` | rendered site for GitHub Pages |
| `.codex/` in this repo | public starter bundle |
| `~/.codex/` | your live local user-level install |
| `~/voice/` | your private voice layer |
| project repo `AGENTS.md` | repo constitution and working rules |
| project repo `.codex-state/` | local checkpoint and resume files |

## The Ditto Rule

Keep the system open to future additions.

That means:

- do not freeze the workflow as if this is the final shape
- document new traits as they get added
- keep the public starter generic
- keep private voice and machine-specific behavior separate
- let the website become the shared memory for the team
